title: Cat Chewing on Electrical Cords: Safety Guide & Triage Tips
meta_desc: Learn why cats chewing wires is a high-priority emergency, the risks of electrical shock, and how photos help your veterinarian with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Electrical cord chewing is a high-priority emergency that requires immediate veterinary evaluation to monitor for life-threatening lung fluid accumulation and internal burns.

What should I know about cats chewing on electrical cords?

Cord chewing is a dangerous behavior where a cat gnaws on live electrical wires. This behavior may stem from several underlying causes:

  • Boredom or lack of enrichment
  • Teething in kittens
  • Dental pain
  • A medical condition called pica

While the rubber coating may seem like a toy to a cat, the internal wires carry a current that can cause devastating internal and external injuries.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my cat chews on a power cord?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, if your cat has chewed an electrical cord, it is a high-priority emergency. Even if your cat appears normal, immediate veterinary evaluation is critical to monitor for:

  • Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, a life-threatening condition where fluid fills the lungs several hours after the shock.
  • Cardiac arrhythmias (irregular heartbeats).
  • Oral burns and tissue necrosis.
  • Acute respiratory distress.

How does taking a photo of the damaged cord help with the triage process?

If it is safe to do so, taking a photo of the damaged cord and your cat’s mouth can significantly assist the triage process in the following ways:

  • A photo of the cord helps the vet understand the potential voltage exposure.
  • A clear image of your cat’s gums and tongue allows the medical team to assess for thermal burns or tissue necrosis.
  • This information helps the clinic prepare necessary oxygen therapy or stabilization equipment before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Chewing on electrical cords is most frequently observed in kittens. Clinical signs include hesitation to eat or drink, drooling, and resentment of handling. Oral examination may reveal a linear scar across the dorsum of the tongue, outlining the path of the electric cord, and scars or wounds at one or both lip commissures. Adjacent carnassial teeth may be discolored and eventually require endodontic treatment. Systemic involvement, such as noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, may be life-threatening and requires immediate evaluation and treatment.

Chapter: Dentistry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 366)

faqs.question: What are the immediate signs of electrical shock in cats?
faqs.answer: Common signs include drooling, difficulty breathing, burnt whiskers, or visible white or charred sores on the tongue and gums.

faqs.question: How can I safely stop my cat from chewing wires?
faqs.answer: Use plastic cord protectors, apply pet-safe bitter-tasting sprays to cables, and ensure your cat has plenty of safe chew toys and mental stimulation.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat is currently touching a live wire?
faqs.answer: Do not touch the cat directly, as you could also be shocked. Turn off the power at the breaker or unplug the cord using a non-conductive object (like a wooden broom handle) before checking your pet.
